title: Spend Before June 30? The Cash Timing Calculation Most SME Owners Skip
linkedin:
Most EOFY spending decisions get made on the tax side of the ledger. The cash impact tends to show up in August.

The timing works like this. When a business settles a material invoice before June 30, the full amount leaves the bank account that day. For most Australian businesses using a tax agent, the income tax return for the year ending June 30 is lodged between October and May of the following year. The tax benefit arrives after that.

The business carries the full cash cost from June through to whenever the tax position is resolved. A $40,000 equipment purchase means $40,000 out in June and a fraction of that amount back, months later.

July arrives with its own weight. Superannuation for the April to June quarter is due by 28 July. Revenue from the new financial year is still building. Any significant June purchase has already reduced the opening working capital balance.

Three categories deserve an honest look before committing: equipment that shifted from low priority in March to urgent in May, stock builds ahead of uncertain demand, and prepaid expenses where the financial year deadline is driving the timing.

A 13-week cash flow forecast projected through September turns the question from abstract to specific. What does the second week of July look like after this purchase? What does August look like? Is there a facility or confirmed receivables pipeline if the gap opens wider?

Owners who run this calculation often make the same purchase, just with staged terms, a short facility, or adjusted timing. Owners who skip it often find the answer in August.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance #AustralianBusiness

When the invoice is settled before June 30, the cash leaves immediately. For most Australian businesses using a tax agent, the income tax return is lodged between October and May of the following year. The tax benefit arrives after that.

google:
For owner-led businesses on the East Coast, the next few weeks carry a specific cash question that is easy to overlook in the June rush.

When a business settles a significant invoice before June 30, the full amount leaves the bank account immediately. The tax benefit arrives later, once the income tax return for the year ending 30 June is lodged and processed. For most Australian businesses working with a tax agent, that falls somewhere between October and May of the following year.

The business carries the full cost through July and August. Those months already have their own demands: the superannuation guarantee for the April to June quarter is due by 28 July, and new financial year revenue is typically still building.

A 13-week cash flow forecast projected through June 30 and into September shows the actual cash position after a purchase commitment. It answers the question that matters before committing: what does July actually look like?

ProfitPulse has published a post covering this calculation for Queensland, New South Wales, and Victorian SME owners, including the three categories of EOFY spending that most often create new-year cash pressure, and what owners who plan ahead do differently.

If significant commitments are on the table before June 30, read this first.
